label: Consumer Council warns of hidden costs and privacy risks at gyms
label_zh: 消費者委員會警告健身房存在隱藏費用和私隱風險

timeline.title: Consumer Council Reports Record Complaints Against 24-Hour Gyms Over Hidden Fees and Biometric Data Collection
timeline.title_zh: 消費者委員會報告 24 小時健身房隱藏費用和生物識別數據收集的投訴創紀錄
timeline.sub_title: Following a surge in consumer grievances, Hong Kong's watchdog investigated 11 fitness chains, uncovering misleading membership pricing, restrictive suspension policies, and intrusive personal data practices such as facial recognition.
timeline.sub_title_zh: 在消費者申訴激增後，香港監管機構調查了 11 家健身連鎖店，發現了誤導性的會員定價、限制性的暫停政策以及侵入性的個人數據實踐，例如面部識別。
timeline.summary:
Hong Kong’s Consumer Council has raised serious alarms over the practices of 24-hour fitness centers, following a record-breaking surge in consumer complaints. Data released on Monday shows that the watchdog logged 155 complaints regarding round-the-clock gyms in 2025, an 86.7% year-on-year increase. This trend has continued into 2026, with 49 complaints received in the first two months alone—more than triple the amount recorded during the same period last year. Most [ref:9] grievances involve disputes over contract cancellations, misleading sales tactics, and complex membership terms that leave consumers financially disadvantaged.

A targeted investigation into 11 major 24-hour gym chains revealed that advertised monthly fees often fail to reflect the total cost of membership. While all surveyed operators offered monthly plans, nearly half imposed non-refundable 'junk fees,' including enrolment charges and mandatory fees for electronic keys or access cards. For short [ref:6, 2, 5]-term users, these additional costs significantly inflate the effective price. In one [ref:5, 6, 2, 11] extreme case, the average monthly cost for a one-month plan reached HK$1,850, more than double the effective monthly rate of a 12-month contract at the same facility. The Council also flagged deceptive advertising, noting that some gyms promoting 'one-month' plans actually required a minimum two-month commitment, while others billed on a 28-day cycle, resulting in 13 payments per year.

Beyond financial transparency, the Council expressed deep concern regarding personal data privacy. The survey found that nine out of the 11 gym chains utilize facial recognition systems for access control, requiring members to provide sensitive biometric images. During trial sessions, several gyms were found to have excessive data collection practices, such as requiring visitors to surrender their physical identity cards or provide full identity card numbers. The watchdog [ref:2, 9] reminded operators that they must adhere to the 'necessary and not excessive' principle of the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance, urging them to consider less intrusive alternatives for identity verification.

Contractual flexibility remains another major pain point for consumers. While most gyms allow for membership suspension under specific circumstances—such as pregnancy, illness, or relocation—the policies vary wildly. Some operators [ref:9] charge monthly handling fees for these 'freezes,' while others require extensive documentation and limit the suspension period to just one or two months. The Council has [ref:9, 6] advised fitness enthusiasts to calculate total annual costs carefully, scrutinize renewal and suspension clauses, and remain cautious about sharing sensitive biometric data before committing to long-term agreements. As the 24-hour [ref:9] fitness sector continues to expand, the watchdog intends to monitor whether these businesses align their marketing and data practices with consumer protection standards.
timeline.summary_zh:
香港消費者委員會就 24 小時健身中心的做法敲響了嚴重警鐘，此前消費者投訴呈創紀錄激增。週一發布的數據顯示，監管機構在 2025 年記錄了 155 起關於全天候健身房的投訴，同比增長 86.7%。這一趨勢持續到 2026 年，僅在頭兩個月就收到了 49 起投訴——是去年同期記錄數量的三倍多。大多數不滿涉及合同取消糾紛、誤導性銷售策略和使消費者處於不利財務地位的複雜會員條款。[ref:9]

對 11 家主要 24 小時健身連鎖店進行的專項調查發現，廣告中的月費通常未能反映會員總成本。雖然所有受訪營運商都提供月度計劃，但近一半的公司收取不可退還的「垃圾費用」，包括註冊費和電子鑰匙或門禁卡的強制收費。對於短期用戶而言，這些額外費用顯著提高了實際價格。在一個極端案例中，一個月計劃的平均月費達到了 1850 港元，是同一健身房 12 個月合同實際月費的兩倍多。該委員會還標記了欺騙性廣告，指出一些健身房推廣「一個月」計劃的實際上要求至少簽訂兩個月的承諾，而其他健身房則以 28 天為週期計費，導致每年有 13 次付款。[ref:6, 2, 5, 11]

除了財務透明度之外，委員會還對個人數據隱私表示深切關注。調查發現，11 家健身連鎖店中有 9 家使用面部識別系統進行門禁控制，要求會員提供敏感的生物識別圖像。在試用期間，一些健身房被發現存在過度的數據收集行為，例如要求訪客交出實體身份證或提供完整的身份證號碼。監管機構提醒營運商，他們必須遵守《個人資料（私隱）條例》的「必要且不過度」原則，敦促他們考慮採用侵入性較低的身份驗證替代方案。[ref:2, 9]

合約彈性仍然是消費者面臨的另一個主要痛點。雖然大多數健身房在特定情況下（例如懷孕、疾病或搬遷）允許暫停會員資格，但政策差異很大。一些營運商對這些「凍結」收取每月手續費，而另一些則需要大量的證明文件，並將暫停期限制為僅一到兩個月。委員會建議健身愛好者仔細計算年度總成本，仔細審查續約和暫停條款，並在承諾長期協議之前謹慎分享敏感的生物識別數據。[ref:9, 6]

timeline.title: Consumer Council Warns of Excessive Hidden Fees and Data Privacy Risks at 24-Hour Gyms
timeline.title_zh: 消費者議會警告24小時健身中心隱藏收費過多及數據私隱風險
timeline.sub_title: A comprehensive review of Hong Kong fitness centers reveals that advertised monthly rates often mask significant non-refundable charges and intrusive personal data collection practices.
timeline.sub_title_zh: 香港健身中心全面檢討，揭露廣告月費常隱藏巨額不可退還費用及侵入性個人數據收集手法。
timeline.summary:
Hong Kong’s Consumer Council has issued a stern warning to fitness enthusiasts regarding the 24-hour gym sector, highlighting a significant discrepancy between advertised prices and the actual costs of membership. Following a detailed investigation into 11 major gym chains operating across the city, the consumer watchdog discovered that many operators utilize complex fee structures and mandatory non-refundable charges that can effectively double the monthly cost for short-term members. This [ref:4, 2] warning comes amid a rapid expansion of the round-the-clock fitness industry, which has grown to accommodate the flexible schedules of modern urban residents but has also drawn increasing scrutiny for its lack of transparency and aggressive data collection.

The Council’s review, which utilized both online research and mystery shoppers at 22 different outlets, found that while all 11 operators advertised flexible monthly plans, nearly half imposed additional costs that were not immediately apparent. These hidden expenses include mandatory enrolment fees, administrative charges, and fees for electronic keys or access cards. In one [ref:2, 4] extreme case, the effective monthly cost for a one-month plan was found to be more than twice the advertised rate once these compulsory fees were factored in. Furthermore [ref:2], the Council noted that some gyms promoting “no-contract” or “one-month” plans actually required a minimum commitment of two months, while others utilized a 28-day billing cycle that results in 13 payments per year rather than the expected 12.

Beyond financial transparency, the watchdog raised serious concerns regarding the collection of sensitive personal data. During the trial phase, some gyms required visitors to surrender their physical identification documents temporarily, a practice the Council deemed excessive. Once enrolled, the majority of operators—nine out of 11—employed facial recognition systems for access control. This requires the collection [ref:2] of biometric facial images, raising questions about the necessity of such intrusive technology for simple gym access. The Council reminded [ref:2] operators that any data collection must adhere to the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance, ensuring that the information gathered is necessary and not excessive for the stated purpose.

The investigation also shed light on the varying policies regarding contract renewals and membership suspensions. Handling charges for freezing [ref:2] a membership varied widely between operators, and some chains had rigid eligibility conditions that made it difficult for consumers to pause their fees during illness or travel. The Council urged consumers to look beyond the headline monthly fee and calculate the total annual cost, including all one-off charges and potential renewal fees, before signing any agreements. 

In response to these findings, the Consumer Council has called on the fitness industry to adopt more consumer-friendly practices, including clearer disclosure of all potential charges and more conservative data collection policies. For consumers, the advice remains to be vigilant: read the fine print of renewal clauses, understand the mechanisms for fee adjustments, and assess whether the convenience of a 24-hour facility outweighs the potential risks to their privacy and wallet.
timeline.summary_zh:
香港消費者議會已向健身愛好者就24小時健身中心行業發出嚴厲警告，強調廣告價格與實際會員費用之間存在重大差異。經過對本港11家主要連鎖健身中心進行詳細調查後，該消費者監管機構發現，許多營運商採用複雜的收費結構及強制性的不可退還收費，這可能使短期會員的月費實際上翻倍。此次警告是在全天候健身行業迅速擴張之際發出的，該行業的發展旨在適應現代城市居民彈性的日程安排，但也因缺乏透明度及侵略性的數據收集行為而日益受到審查。[ref:4, 2]

消費者議會的檢討結合了線上研究及在22家不同門市進行的神秘顧客調查，發現儘管所有11家營運商均宣傳靈活的月度計劃，但近一半的公司施加了不顯眼的額外費用。這些隱藏費用包括強制性註冊費、行政費以及電子鑰匙或門禁卡費用。在一個極端案例中，在將這些強制性費用計入後，一個月度計劃的實際月費比廣告費高出一倍以上。此外，消費者議會指出，一些推廣「無合約」或「一個月」計劃的健身中心實際上要求至少簽約兩個月，而另一些則採用28天的計費週期，導致每年有13次付款而非預期的12次。[ref:2, 4]

除了財務透明度，該監管機構還對敏感個人數據的收集提出了嚴重關切。在試用階段，一些健身中心要求訪客暫時交出實體身份證明文件，消費者議會認為此舉過於嚴苛。一旦註冊，大多數營運商（11家中有9家）採用面部識別系統進行門禁控制。這需要收集生物識別面部圖像，引發了對這種侵入性技術對於簡單健身中心門禁的必要性問題。消費者議會提醒營運商，任何數據收集都必須遵守《個人資料（私隱）條例》，確保收集的資訊對於所述目的而言是必需的，而非過度。[ref:2]

此次調查還揭示了關於合約續約及會員暫停辦理的各類政策。會員凍結費用的處理方式在營運商之間差異很大，有些連鎖店設有嚴格的資格條件，使得消費者在生病或旅行期間難以暫停繳費。消費者議會敦促消費者在簽署任何協議前，應仔細查看標題月費之外的資訊，並計算包括所有一次性費用及潛在續約費在內的總年費。

針對這些調查結果，消費者議會呼籲健身業界採取更具消費者友善的實踐，包括更清晰地披露所有潛在收費及採取更審慎的數據收集政策。對消費者而言，建議依然是保持警惕：仔細閱讀續約條款的細節，理解費用調整機制，並評估24小時健身設施的便利性是否超過其潛在的財務及私隱風險。
